name: African Lion
description: A powerful big cat that lives in prides and hunts large herbivores across African grasslands and savannas.
fun_fact: Lions are the only big cats that commonly live in social groups called prides.
lifespan: 10–14 years in the wild; up to 20 years in captivity
size: Body length 1.4–2.5 m; shoulder height about 1.0–1.2 m; weight 120–250 kg

name: Bengal Tiger
description: A large striped cat native to South Asia, known for strength, stealth, and solitary hunting.
fun_fact: The Bengal tiger is one of the largest living cat subspecies.
lifespan: 10–15 years in the wild; up to 20 years in captivity
size: Body length 2.4–3.1 m including tail; weight 100–260 kg

name: Asian Elephant
description: A highly intelligent elephant species found in forests and grasslands across South and Southeast Asia.
fun_fact: Asian elephants communicate using low-frequency sounds that can travel long distances.
lifespan: 48–60 years in the wild; sometimes longer in captivity

name: African Bush Elephant
description: The largest living land animal, recognized by its huge ears, tusks, and social family herds.
fun_fact: Elephants can use their trunks like a hand, nose, snorkel, and drinking tool.
natural_enemies: Crocodiles may threaten calves near water

name: Giant Panda
description: A black-and-white bear that mainly eats bamboo and lives in mountain forests of central China.
fun_fact: A giant panda may spend more than half the day eating bamboo.
lifespan: 15–20 years in the wild; up to 30 years in captivity
natural_enemies: Snow leopards may prey on cubs

name: Red Panda
description: A small tree-dwelling mammal with reddish fur and a long ringed tail, found in Himalayan forests.
fun_fact: Red pandas use their bushy tails like blankets in cold mountain weather.
lifespan: 8–10 years in the wild; up to 15 years in captivity
size: Body length 50–64 cm; tail 28–59 cm; weight 3–6 kg

name: Polar Bear
description: A large Arctic bear adapted to hunting seals on sea ice.
fun_fact: Polar bear skin is black under its white-looking fur.
lifespan: 15–25 years in the wild; up to 30 years in captivity

name: Gray Wolf
description: A social canid that lives and hunts in packs across forests, tundra, and grasslands.
fun_fact: Wolf howls help pack members communicate across long distances.
lifespan: 6–8 years in the wild; up to 13 years or more in protected areas
size: Body length 1–1.6 m; shoulder height 66–81 cm; weight 23–80 kg

name: Cheetah
description: The fastest land animal, built for short high-speed chases in open habitats.
fun_fact: Cheetahs cannot roar like lions; they chirp, purr, and hiss.
lifespan: 10–12 years in the wild; up to 17 years in captivity

name: Snow Leopard
description: A secretive mountain cat with thick fur and a long tail for balance and warmth.
fun_fact: Snow leopards can leap several times their body length across rocky terrain.
lifespan: 10–12 years in the wild; up to 20 years in captivity
size: Body length 90–115 cm; tail 80–105 cm; weight 22–55 kg

name: Koala
description: A tree-living marsupial that feeds mostly on eucalyptus leaves.
fun_fact: Koalas sleep many hours a day because eucalyptus leaves are low in energy.

name: Kangaroo
description: A hopping marsupial known for powerful hind legs, a balancing tail, and carrying young in a pouch.
fun_fact: Kangaroos use their tails like a fifth limb when moving slowly.
lifespan: 8–12 years in the wild; up to 20 years in captivity
size: Height 1–2 m depending on species; weight 18–90 kg

name: Platypus
description: An egg-laying mammal with a duck-like bill that hunts underwater for small aquatic animals.
fun_fact: Male platypuses have venomous spurs on their hind legs.
size: Body length 38–60 cm including tail; weight 0.7–2.4 kg

name: Sloth
description: A slow-moving tree-dwelling mammal found in Central and South American forests.
fun_fact: Sloths have algae growing in their fur, which can help camouflage them.
lifespan: 20–30 years in the wild depending on species

name: Orangutan
description: A highly intelligent great ape with long arms, found in the rainforests of Borneo and Sumatra.
fun_fact: Orangutans build a new sleeping nest in trees almost every night.
lifespan: 30–40 years in the wild; up to 50 years or more in captivity

name: Gorilla
description: The largest living primate, known for strong family groups led by a silverback male.
fun_fact: Gorillas use many vocalizations, gestures, and facial expressions to communicate.
lifespan: 35–40 years in the wild; up to 50 years in captivity
natural_enemies: Leopards may prey on young or weak individuals

name: Giraffe
description: The tallest living land animal, using its long neck to browse leaves high in trees.
fun_fact: A giraffe has the same number of neck vertebrae as a human: seven.

name: Hippopotamus
description: A large semi-aquatic mammal that spends much of the day in water and grazes at night.
fun_fact: Hippos secrete a reddish oily fluid that helps protect their skin.
size: Length 3.3–5 m; shoulder height 1.3–1.6 m; weight 1,300–3,200 kg

name: Black Rhinoceros
description: A browsing rhinoceros with a hooked upper lip used to feed on shrubs and trees.
fun_fact: Black rhinos are browsers, while white rhinos are mostly grazers.
size: Shoulder height 1.4–1.8 m; length 3–3.8 m; weight 800–1,400 kg

name: Meerkat
description: A small mongoose that lives in groups and uses sentinels to watch for predators.
fun_fact: Meerkats take turns standing guard while others forage.
lifespan: 6–10 years in the wild; up to 12 years in captivity
size: Body length 25–35 cm; tail 17–25 cm; weight 0.6–1 kg

name: Emperor Penguin
description: The largest penguin species, famous for breeding during the harsh Antarctic winter.
fun_fact: Male emperor penguins keep eggs warm on their feet under a brood pouch.

name: Bald Eagle
description: A large sea eagle of North America, recognized by its white head and tail.
fun_fact: Bald eagles often steal fish from other birds, a behavior called kleptoparasitism.
size: Body length 70–102 cm; wingspan 1.8–2.3 m; weight 3–6.3 kg
natural_enemies: Great horned owls may attack nests

name: Harpy Eagle
description: A powerful rainforest eagle that hunts monkeys, sloths, and other tree-dwelling animals.
fun_fact: The harpy eagle has enormous talons that help it catch large prey.
size: Body length 86–107 cm; wingspan 1.8–2.2 m; weight 4–9 kg
natural_enemies: Jaguars or large snakes may threaten nestlings

name: Snowy Owl
description: A pale Arctic owl that hunts lemmings and other small animals in open tundra.
fun_fact: Unlike many owls, snowy owls are often active during the day.
lifespan: 10 years in the wild; longer in captivity
size: Body length 52–71 cm; wingspan 1.25–1.5 m; weight 1.6–3 kg
natural_enemies: Jaegers and gulls may attack chicks

name: Scarlet Macaw
description: A bright red, yellow, and blue parrot that lives in tropical forests of Central and South America.
fun_fact: Scarlet macaws use strong beaks to crack hard nuts and seeds.
lifespan: 30–50 years in the wild; up to 70 years in captivity

name: Flamingo
description: A long-legged wading bird known for pink plumage caused by pigments in its food.
fun_fact: Flamingos often feed upside down by filtering tiny organisms from water.
lifespan: 20–30 years in the wild; longer in captivity
size: Height 80–145 cm; wingspan 1–1.7 m; weight 2–4 kg
natural_enemies: Large gulls may take eggs or chicks

name: Ostrich
description: The largest living bird, flightless but able to run at high speeds on open land.
fun_fact: Ostriches have the largest eggs of any living bird.
lifespan: 30–40 years in the wild; up to 50 years in captivity
natural_enemies: Jackals may take eggs and chicks

name: Great Hornbill
description: A large forest bird with a huge yellow bill and casque, important for spreading fruit seeds.
fun_fact: Female hornbills seal themselves inside tree cavities while nesting.
lifespan: 35–40 years in the wild; up to 50 years in captivity
size: Body length 95–120 cm; wingspan about 1.5 m; weight 2–4 kg

name: Komodo Dragon
description: The largest living lizard, native to several Indonesian islands.
fun_fact: Komodo dragons use a keen sense of smell to locate carrion from far away.
size: Length 2–3 m; weight 70–90 kg or more
natural_enemies: Adult Komodo dragons may prey on juveniles

name: Green Sea Turtle
description: A marine turtle that migrates long distances between feeding areas and nesting beaches.
fun_fact: Adult green sea turtles mostly graze on seagrasses and algae.
natural_enemies: Crabs and birds prey on hatchlings

name: Leatherback Sea Turtle
description: The largest sea turtle, with a leathery shell and a diet mainly of jellyfish.
fun_fact: Leatherbacks can dive deeper than many other marine reptiles.
natural_enemies: Birds and crabs prey on hatchlings

name: King Cobra
description: The world's longest venomous snake, known for raising its hood when threatened.
fun_fact: King cobras mainly eat other snakes.

name: American Alligator
description: A large freshwater crocodilian found in wetlands of the southeastern United States.
fun_fact: Alligators create wetland holes that help other animals during dry periods.
lifespan: 35–50 years in the wild; up to 70 years in captivity
size: Length 2.6–4.6 m; weight 90–450 kg depending on sex and age
natural_enemies: Birds and large fish may take young

name: Galápagos Tortoise
description: A giant island tortoise native to the Galápagos Islands, famous for long lifespan and large size.
fun_fact: Some Galápagos tortoises can live for more than 100 years.
size: Shell length up to 1.2 m; weight 150–250 kg or more
natural_enemies: Feral pigs and dogs threaten young

name: Chameleon
description: A family of lizards known for independently moving eyes, long tongues, and color-changing ability.
fun_fact: Many chameleons change color for communication and temperature control, not just camouflage.

name: Poison Dart Frog
description: Small, brightly colored frogs whose skin toxins warn predators to stay away.
fun_fact: Some poison dart frogs get toxins from chemicals in the insects they eat.
natural_enemies: Birds may avoid brightly colored species

name: Axolotl
description: A salamander that keeps larval features as an adult and is famous for regeneration.
fun_fact: Axolotls can regrow limbs and parts of organs.
lifespan: 10–15 years in captivity; shorter in the wild

name: Great White Shark
description: A large predatory shark found in cool coastal waters around the world.
fun_fact: Great white sharks can detect tiny electrical signals produced by other animals.
size: Length 4–6 m; weight 680–1,900 kg or more

name: Whale Shark
description: The largest living fish, a gentle filter feeder that eats plankton and small fish.
fun_fact: Whale sharks have spot patterns that are unique to each individual.
size: Length 8–12 m commonly; can exceed 18 m
natural_enemies: Orcas may attack young or weak individuals
Large sharks may prey on juveniles

name: Clownfish
description: Small reef fish known for living among the stinging tentacles of sea anemones.
fun_fact: Clownfish and sea anemones have a mutual relationship that helps both survive.

name: Blue Tang
description: A bright blue reef fish with a yellow tail, often found in Indo-Pacific coral reefs.
fun_fact: Blue tangs have sharp spines near their tails for defense.

name: Manta Ray
description: A giant ray that glides through warm oceans while filtering plankton from the water.
fun_fact: Manta rays have the largest brain-to-body ratio of any fish.
size: Wingspan 3–7 m depending on species; weight can exceed 1,000 kg

name: Blue Whale
description: The largest animal known to have ever lived, feeding mainly on tiny krill.
fun_fact: A blue whale's heart can be about the size of a small car.

name: Bottlenose Dolphin
description: An intelligent marine mammal known for social groups, echolocation, and playful behavior.
fun_fact: Dolphins use signature whistles that may function like individual names.

name: Sea Otter
description: A marine mammal that floats on its back and uses tools to open shellfish.
fun_fact: Sea otters have extremely dense fur that keeps them warm in cold water.

name: Octopus
description: A soft-bodied marine animal with eight arms, high intelligence, and excellent camouflage.
fun_fact: Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood.
size: Arm span from 30 cm to several meters depending on species

name: Monarch Butterfly
description: A famous orange-and-black butterfly known for long-distance migration across North America.
fun_fact: Monarch caterpillars feed on milkweed, which helps make them distasteful to predators.
lifespan: 2–6 weeks for most adults; migrating generation up to 8–9 months
natural_enemies: Birds such as black-headed grosbeaks and orioles

name: Praying Mantis
description: A predatory insect with folded front legs used to grab prey.
fun_fact: Praying mantises can rotate their heads more than many other insects.

name: Tarantula
description: A large hairy spider family found in warm regions around the world.
fun_fact: Some tarantulas flick irritating hairs from their abdomen as a defense.
lifespan: Males 5–10 years; females 15–30 years depending on species
size: Body length 5–11 cm; leg span up to 25–30 cm

name: Horseshoe Crab
description: An ancient marine arthropod with a hard shell and long tail spine.
fun_fact: Horseshoe crabs are more closely related to spiders than to true crabs.
size: Body length 45–60 cm including tail; weight 1–5 kg
